Yeah.

Smart.
Yeah.

She

Okay, yeah, I'm in Austin about, about three hours south of Dallas.
Yeah, yeah, nice.
Hey team, nice to see you.
Just to introduce myself really quickly, I was not at the kickoff call.

Uh, like I was just telling Jody, we like to keep kickoffs pretty small, um, to save time on intros, but, um, I'm a senior managing editor at growthx, I've been here since December, uh, prior to joining, I was working as a freelance content marketer for brands like SEMBrush and Segment, um, before that I was at another content marketing agency called Animals, so, I have a lot of experience, um, building content strategies for B2B and B2C SaaS companies, um, at Growthx, I'm working with Marcel and

Team to onboard our new customers and get you set up with a content strategy and also set a benchmark in terms of content quality so that, you know, everything that we're producing with our AI workflows looks the way you want it to.
And, you know, it's content that you're happy to publish on your website.
Awesome.
Thank you.
Nice to meet you.
Well, we can introduce ourselves to An Alberto, run marketing.
Mauricio, or Mal for short, and I work on Alberto's team as head of product go-to-market.
Atul, I'm the product marketing manager, part of the marketing team.
Sounds like you and Jody were already...
Mm But he's one of our technical gurus and sales gurus.
Do we need to wait for anyone else, or is it just...
No, it's just going to be me today, so I understand it's going to be a bit of a product...
I have some questions, but I'm happy to keep them for the end.
Yeah, so the goal, as you guys asked, in the Slack channel, was to expose it to the platform.
And we invited Jody because he's been here for the longest and also does demos to customers on a regular basis and was an operator on the ground in his past.
So he knows the personas really, really well as well.
So we could start from there.
I don't know how much, or do you need any, before we go into product demo, do you need any additional introduction and context?
No, no, I've already kind of, I've, I watched the kickoff call.
I, you know,
I've about the company, so I have enough context, I think, to get started.
Cool.
Then why don't we dive in, and then we'll dig in from there.
Joey, I'll turn it over to you, just like I would do what you normally do with a new customer.
Okay, great.
Well, Aida, what you're looking at on the screen is an ambient web application.

And if you're in the security industry as one of our potential prospects, this looks quite a bit different from the standard video surveillance system interface that they're all used to over the years.
Traditionally speaking, a video surveillance interface would oftentimes open and present a lot of camera views, their live streams.

It used to be very useful because you had no other choice but to have a human being sit down and look at the live video to make sure that things were safe and that people were following the rules and whatnot.
But it was also extremely labor-intensive, time-intensive.

Intensive, had a very high rate of failures because humans have been proven over time, they lose focus, the attention decrement becomes a problem, they can't focus on multiple streams all at once, etc.

So now we're at this point where AI technology has evolved that we feel that we can confidently replace much of that requirement from a human's perspective and refocus the human attention onto more strategic or more tactical goals within the security organization's operations.
So what we do instead is we present what we call the Pulse Tab, and the Pulse Tab is literally that.
It is the pulse of activity occurring within a facility.

It's the kind of actionable data that is useful for a security operator to look at because it's only moments of their attention, and it's showing them some sort of activity that's related to the profiles, the threat profiles that their organization are interested in.
So what it does, it takes low severity level events.
And it presents them on a screen without any kind of audible alarm.
The screen can cue, so these events can just rotate through as they occur.
These are all low-severy-level events, so they're not necessarily like code red, where an operator needs to respond very quickly.
It's more like situational awareness, nice-to-know kind of function.
And if I'm the operator, I can very easily just clear the alarm if it's nothing to worry about, so I just can resolve it.

Or maybe I do actually want to jump in and view the alert with a little bit more detail, see what this activity really is, see who's involved, what are they up to, watch a live camera feed of that specific event occurring.
Or maybe I immediately recognize that this actually is not a low-severy-level event.
This is something that does require a response, and so I can automatically elevate it.

And what's really cool here is the moment I click that button, the AI can take over and automate my entire workflow to ensure that all of the right people are being contacted and alerted to this activity occurring.
And then our...
Operating procedures are being followed accordingly.
It's all automated.
Now, another really important part of this architecture, in the security industry, trust is paramount.

When it comes to trusting your video surveillance technology, you have to make sure that the noise, the signals that you're receiving are not noise, because that involves a lot of false responses, a lot of time and effort, manpower, and cost.
And oftentimes, the signals are the most, excuse me, oftentimes, most of the signals are just noise.
They're not actual security events, because of the nature of how the technology works.

So you need some sort of mechanism that understands the difference between something that is a low severity level event that doesn't need to be notified to the entire department versus something that is a medium or higher severity level event that maybe does require more attention and a greater response.
So we want to utilize the AI to be able to do that.
And we start with this Pulse tab.
The Pulse tab is showing the low severity situational type events occurring in real time.
We're contextualizing those with the AI.
So the AI is actually able to understand that not only is this a person loitering event, it's a person loitering standing outside of the building.
It's able to describe the color of clothing they're wearing, what their general activity is, and then whether or not this is currently posing a security risk.
Now, that's a very common kind of detection in the security industry, person loitering.
It's one of the most common types of analytics that you could use.
And it's very impactful, but it's also one of the noisiest.
So when you add the context, now you have the ability to inform the AI.
If the context changes to a certain degree, we want to automatically elevate that risk.
We want now to bring in a broader team.
We don't want a human to have to do that contextualization step, though, because...
Because it's too slow, and it still doesn't solve the manpower problem.
So we want to use the AI to do that.
So something as simple as person loitering can very quickly escalate to a different kind of event when the context changes.
It may be something like this.
Graffiti attacking on well detected.
This is still person loitering.
That's the base threat signature that the AI was able to detect.

But then when the AI assesses the context, which happens very quickly, within milliseconds, the AI now understands not only is this a person loitering, but this is a vandalism activity, graffiti behavior, and we can even detect the spray paint can in the individual's hand.
This allows the AI to then rename the threat signature instead of person loitering.
It says, okay, this is graffiti tagging on wall, which for me as a security operator, that's immediately impactful and helpful.
Because now I understand that person loitering is something I need to act on immediately.
I don't have to see an alert that says person loitering and then...
Look at the video, try and figure out what's going on, make a judgment call, and then start the operating procedure.
The AI has already started it for me.
And of course, if the context changes even further, even more dramatically, the AI can escalate to a higher severity level event.
Masked individuals with crowbars detected.
Now, still person loitering, but the context is quite a bit different.
We have individuals wearing masks, break-in behavior, tools associated to break-in behavior.
The AI understands that this is a much more severe level event, is able to escalate it, and then rename it to something that immediately provides context to the operators.
Now, at this point, there's a lot of tools that are embedded into the technology to ensure that the operation is efficient to the response as possible.
So we give you geolocation capabilities to show you exactly where that alert's coming from.
A time-looped GIF that plays over and over again, so you don't miss any of the important details of the alert.
A timeline that's capturing all the important details.
Communications, notes, comments that are being made between the operators and the responders makes my after-action reporting as a security leader much more efficient.
I can get all of my information in one place, and I know exactly how the event unfolded and how my team responded.
I've got all the important details of the event.
And then just like the old-fashioned video management system, we have the ability to go back and watch historical video.
We can make clips, and we can save them in an archive service.
All of the quote-unquote glovebox features that you find in a video surveillance system today, we do that.
We just add all the intelligence on top of it as well to make it way more powerful.
Now, this extrapolates out into a lot of really powerful features.

I'll start with just a couple of additional detections, and then I'll show you some things that we do around AI-powered forensics, which is the ability to investigate in real time and get more information instead of sifting through hours and hours of video.
But the detections thing is oftentimes what we talk about, especially…
Right now with what's going on in the U.S.
around gun violence, it's about preventing active shooter events or at least the ability to detect a person with a firearm as early as possible.
And we believe that that is an extremely valuable detection.

But we also know that if that's all you do and if that's all you're concerned with, you're actually too late already because the moment that someone pulls out a handgun is only moments away from someone being injured or potentially killed.
So instead, what we want to do is we want to look for all this other kind of behavior that generally either proceeds or accompanies those kinds of events.
It starts with something simple like a vehicle loitering that you see here.
An operator could very quickly say, this vehicle, I don't recognize it, it shouldn't be in a parking lot, and they could start to investigate.
And then they would very quickly realize that this guy is up to no good.
Or maybe it's somebody loitering in front of a doorway waiting for that perfect moment to slip in unnoticed.
And, of course, oftentimes it could be somebody.
Actually breaching the perimeter on foot, like jumping over a fence.
Person jumping fence detected.

Now you'll notice that's automatically a medium severity level event because we work with our customers to define something that we call a threat model, which is essentially their measure of risk captured in a multitude of threat signatures that we then compile together and we enable on their system to ensure that all of the things that they're concerned about from a risk profile standpoint are being detected as quickly as possible.
In this case, the customer wants to know if somebody's jumping over one of their fences.
We detect that and we alert it immediately as a medium severity level event.
And then, of course, this guy waiting in front of the door, he's waiting because he wants that perfect moment to slip in.
He does that through a tailgate event.
Tailgating access verified, detected.
Now this also highlights another really powerful aspect of our product, and that is our integration with another technology called access control.
You probably have a card that you have to badge into when you go to
Go to your office.
That's the access control system.
We integrate through that system to understand the context of what's happening at those access control doors.
So in this case, there's three or four individuals that are walking in, and three of them have badged in.
They have valid access.
They're allowed to be in the building.
But then the fourth person does not badge in.
We receive information from the access control system showing us that those three people badged in.
But we use computer vision to understand that four people actually crossed through that secured threshold.
So now we know that one of these people did not badge in, and we're alerting the team that there's an unauthorized intruder in their facility.
Now, once he's in the building, you can't rely on a camera being everywhere, which is another reason why you can't just detect weapons.
You have to be able to detect all these other behaviors.
A lot of times, when something like an active shooter event is unfolding, it starts with something as simple as somebody falling down.
A person recognizes trouble.
They're trying to run away.
If they're like me, when they try to run, they slip and they fall.
That's what happens here.
Or maybe it's a medical emergency.
Or maybe somebody's been pushed or shoved by another person trying to get away.
Whatever it is, when a body hits the floor, that's generally something that a security team wants to know about.
Another really common behavior that you see in these kind of active shooter incidents, large crowds of people just simply trying to get away.
At my office, unless it's Taco Tuesday, you don't ever have a large group of people rushing through the exit door to get down to the lobby.
This is abnormal behavior.
This could be a fire.
It could be a person with a firearm.
It doesn't matter.
The fact that it's abnormal behavior needs to be notified to the security team.
And now they're aware that something's unfolding.
And then, of course, when that detection occurs...
...person brandishing firearm detected.
Immediately a high-severity level event.
We're going through automated workflows to ensure...
I'm all of the right people are being contacted.
We do push notifications directly to their mobile phones.
We do text messages.
We even do phone calls where an automated message will play in someone's ear, and we send out emails.

We can do all those simultaneously, or we can follow an escalation pathway that basically says if Jody receives a text message and he doesn't look at that text message within a set period of time, automatically escalate to these other groups of people to ensure that this incident is being acknowledged and responded to.
Now, there's a host of other threat signatures that we provide.
There's really well over 100 within our threat library.

Each customer uses a different combination of those threat signatures and what we said earlier about the threat model, but there are other aspects of the product that a customer see a lot of value in, and one of those is the AI-powered forensics.
So traditionally speaking, if something happened and I needed to gather evidence, because I did not at that time,
Have a technology that could alert me that that thing happened.
So I'm going retroactively to find evidence to prosecute or to solve a crime or to whatever it may be.
I would log into my video management system.
I would pick a couple of cameras, get as much information as I can as to the who, what, and when and where.
And then I would start going through my cameras and trying to find this person and this event and this activity.
Very tedious.
In fact, if you talk to professional investigators that have done this for a career, they'll tell you that it's the most time-consuming part of their job.
It's simply sitting down and piecing together all this video to make a case or to solve a crime or to prove what happened.
We're going to make that a lot faster by doing something that you and I already do really well.
And that's converse, like human beings.
We just talk to each other.
We need to make that same interface as usable as it is when we're having a discussion whenever I'm talking with an AI product.
And so we've incorporated that with talk to text.
Show me a person.

Carrying a laptop at the front door yesterday afternoon, AI understands exactly what I'm saying, it understands the context of what I'm actually looking for, and then it presents me with all of the results that immediately have some sort of valuable aspect of the context that I'm looking for.
So now I'm looking at video of people at my front door yesterday afternoon carrying laptops in their hands.
I went from the potential of hours of investigation to just a few moments of finding the clip that I needed by simply talking directly to the AI.
Now I can also type in if I'd like, like maybe I want to see a person in a red shirt.

The AI can go through all of my cameras in a matter of moments, find all of the people wearing red shirts, and then I'm able to very quickly say, hey, this guy looks like the guy that matches the description.
So now I I
I want to look in and figure out where else this guy's been in my facility.
And we utilize that same technology, AI-powered forensics, without doing any kind of personally identifiable information.
That's an important part of privacy by design.
And it's a foundation for AI that you can trust, for responsible AI.
So you'll notice this guy's wearing a mask.
We're not doing any facial recognition here.
But we can click on this guy, and the AI can go and find him everywhere across my camera footprint to ensure that I can track his activity and see what else he's been up to.
And then I can create a timeline very quickly and easily.
I just want to add this clip.
I want to add this one.
Let's add this one.
And let's go ahead and add one more just for fun.
Maybe it's this one.
And so now you see over here on the right, I have an exact timeline of everywhere he's been up to the last second that he passed in front of a camera.
This is an extremely powerful tool for an investigator, not only because of the ability to do the talk to text and the natural language search.
Weed out all that wasted time searching through video and instead just look at the stuff that's actionable.
But now I can actually go in and use my cameras to stop crimes from happening in real time by understanding where these people are in my facility and things like who else are they meeting with?
Maybe there's a second suspect in this incident.
Maybe they dropped a package somewhere.
Maybe they stole something else off somebody's desk.
I can see all of their activity very quickly and easily.
And then the last thing that I'll show you.
This is all possible for a multitude of different types of tools related to forensics.
So, for example, as an investigator, one of the first things that you have to do when you're investigating a crime is establish a timeline.

And establishing a timeline can be pretty difficult because you're either relying on witnesses who are actually looking at their watch when that thing happened, which is rare, or you're going back on video and you're spending, again, a lot of time scrubbing through the video to find that moment where this event...
Start it.
So imagine that in this scenario.
I've got a vehicle that's parked in my parking lot.
Nobody knows who it belongs to.
Nobody knows how long it's been there.
Nobody knows if anybody's gotten out of that vehicle or gotten into that vehicle.
And when I ask my security team, hey, what's going on with this car?
You know, they all stop looking at their phone for a second, and then they realize there's a car that's been there for a couple of hours.
Again, because of attention decrement, they can't track what's really happening in real time all the time.
So I can either scrub through the video the old-fashioned way, waste a lot of time, or I can use my AI-powered tools, and with two clicks, I can run a very powerful tool called segmentation.
Segmentation extracts all of the shapes from an image.
Once we can extract the shape of something from an image, we can apply machine learning to understand what that thing is.
We know that this is a car, because the AI has been trained on a vast multitude of data sets related to cars.
And then we already know some very important tools, because we
We spent a lot of time researching and talking to our customers to understand what their operations require, what their day-to-day workflows look like, and what kind of challenges they're addressing.
In this case, I'm an investigator.
I need to establish my timeline.
What better way to establish my timeline than knowing exactly when this car arrived?
I click one button, and I'm presented with a video clip that shows me the moment that that vehicle pulled up.
Then I can take it a step further, and I can say, okay, well, when did a person get out of this vehicle?
When was there a person near this car?
And now I'm presented with clips of when people were getting in or getting out of these vehicles.
And then we could even apply a grid search.
Maybe we found a suspicious package, or maybe somebody lost their wallet.
I want to know, when was there somebody over in this area?
So I do a quick grid search, and I say, show me person.
And then I'm presented with clips of exactly when somebody was in that area.
In this case, he even bends down and is messing with something in that area.
So he's probably the person that dropped his wallet.
Or left left out.
Now these are just two of the main products that we offer through what we call the intelligence platform that Ambient is.
There's actually a lot more.
Part of that is the signals intelligence that I briefly covered, the access control integration piece, the forensics piece.

But then where a lot of our customers find a hidden value that they did not expect is in data, metrics, insights, the ability to power the cameras to do a whole heck of a lot more than just timestamp video.
We want to use these cameras that are almost ubiquitous.
almost everywhere.
Let's use them to make more intelligent data-driven decisions as well, while maintaining privacy for our colleagues, our employees, or whoever it is that's in that facility.
So we have to have a privacy-first technology that can still capture all this really important information.
So then it becomes things like, where are we seeing the most alerts occurring?
How long does it take for our operators to respond to these alerts?
Do I have certain teams that seem to be taking much longer than other teams?
Are there things that are happening?
Happening in my facilities that are causing these types of events.
Like I can go in and I can see exactly how many smoke and fire events occurred.
I can even click on video and look at those exact events and go through them if I need to make a recorded perspective from that.
But what's really cool is, again, we want to make this as intuitive as possible.
Like for me, I don't want to have to be a data scientist to wade through dashboards to go find important information.
I'm the kind of person that just asks a colleague, hey, can you help me get information?
My boss is wanting to know how many tailgate events occurred last week at my facility.
Normally, I'd ask a colleague that was smarter than me and they would go through this dashboard and go find all that information.
Instead, now I'm just going to talk directly to the AI.
How many tailgate alerts occurred last week?
Or I could say something like in the past three days, how many tailgate events occurred?
I could say something like where are the most tailgate alerts occurring?
Like all the kind of.
Questions that are leading towards those insights that I'm looking for.
have the ability to grab that information immediately.
There were 31 tailgate alerts that occurred last week.
We see the time frame here.
And then we can track these alerts if we need to just by clicking on them and looking at them.
And then that helps us train our team.
And then lastly, the most important part or one of the most important parts about the implementation of this product is about cost savings.
There's a lot of expense in security that is not necessarily justifiable now, knowing what this technology can accomplish for your operations.
Security is also one of the most tightly budgeted parts of an organization.
The department in security oftentimes has to go and beg for money because most organizations look at security as a cost center.
And unless something bad happens, they don't really understand what security does.
So it's very difficult to go find money.
One of the best things that we can do is help our customers understand where those gaps are in their operations.
some additional organizations.
Uh, yeah.
I It's be
Where we can save them some money.
And this is the number one thing that we can show them that really highlights that.

This is the access control integration and what we call noise mitigation or signals intelligence, which is basically filtering out all the signals that don't matter to the customer, which is a large amount of access control events that occur.
We can signal, we can filter those out so that the operators don't have to manage them anymore.
That saves the customers a lot of money.

In this example, imagine if you had 109 events that occurred and it cost you $1 in soft labor costs to have an operator look at that event, manage it, make a note, ensure that it wasn't something dangerous.
That all takes time.
Time is money.
If we could have a technology that would automatically eliminate 95 out of the 109 events, thus saving you $95, and this is one door in one day.
Most organizations have thousands of access.
It's this control doors.

That's thousands, if not millions of dollars saved just by implementing this technology, completely separate from the threat detections that we do, completely separate from the efficiencies that we unravel or that we find within the forensics and investigation tools.
This is simply a byproduct of using our technology automatically starts to save organizations a lot of money.
I'll pause there for any questions or comments.
What are the most common, like, threat detections that your customers use?
Like, I'm sure that there are a couple, like, detecting guns or, you know, that are really common, but are there, like, packages that kind of go together?
Yeah, almost universally, it starts with insecurity.
We oftentimes talk about layers.
There's multiple layers as you move closer and closer within the facility, and it always starts with the perimeter as the outside layer.
So almost universally, all of our customers are starting with some form of perimeter protection and awareness.

People jumping fences, vehicles entering into areas they're not supposed to be or entering in the wrong direction, unauthorized vehicles entering, people tailgating or loitering outside of doors or outside of man gates, people loitering outside of fences.
And then as you move in past that perimeter layer, we also start to now think about our employees and our colleagues and life, safety, and health kind of events.
So things like people falling down, somebody slips on ice in the parking lot.
That can also apply to somebody dropping down to crawl underneath a car and steal a catalytic converter.
That's a big problem in certain areas.
And organizations are now tasked with trying to protect their parking lot so their employees aren't scared to come to work.
As you move further and further in, you start to see tailgating is very popular because that's the problem that is almost...
It's universal across the security organization.
We have a threat signature that is based around people interacting with secured assets.
Almost every organization has some sort of asset on a wall, in a cabinet, locked away behind a door, and only certain people are supposed to be messing with that.
Think of a pharmacy in a cabinet full of high-octane medication.
Only the pharmacist is supposed to open that cabinet.
If somebody else walks over there and starts interacting with that cabinet, you want to know about it.
So we can alert for that kind of activity.
That can also be an early warning system for something like a person having a medical emergency.
The first thing that a lot of people do before they even call 911 is they reach for the defibrillator on the wall.
Or at a fire, they reach for a fire extinguisher.
And before that 911 call goes to the fire department, they're already attempting to put out the fire.
We can detect that kind of activity occurring and then be an early warning system for something like a fire.
And, of course, we detect smoke and fire as well.
So a lot of times...

A for organizations that have facilities within big urban areas, a lot of times when facilities have, when organizations have facilities in big urban areas, they have to worry about what's going on around their facility, and unfortunately right now, that can be transient activity, homeless people, encampments, they start fires in a dumpster that can very quickly spread over to their facility, so they want to be at least aware of that kind of thing occurring.
To prepare to mitigate it if it spreads.
And then universally, it's person branching, firearm, people fighting, any kind of aggressive, violent behavior, organizations want to be detected towards that.
And then past that, it really becomes very customized based off of the specific use case that the customer is looking at.
And a lot of times that's based off of a vertical, like if they're in critical infrastructure and energy, smoke and fire is going to be almost paramount.
On their detection list, or, you know, the protection of a fence that secures critical infrastructure like a power plant, that's going to be crucial.
Yeah, yeah, it makes sense.
What are some common trigger points that you see with customers that come to you?
Is there a specific event that makes them realize, okay, we need to upgrade our security?
Or is it, do they, you know, discover you through other ways?
It's primarily being driven, in my opinion, I'll maybe defer to the guys in the room a little bit for some of their input as well.
But in my opinion, it's driven by current events that are happening in the news.
Oftentimes, what an organization does is when something happens to another organization, they say, that's terrible.
Let's pretend it happened to us.
How are we going to react?
How would we have responded?
How would we have managed this event?
And inevitably,
Alternatively, the security guy raises his hand.
The security person raises their hand and says, you know, we need better security first and foremost.
So generally, after some sort of major type event occurs, you see a raise in funding or budgeting for security organizations to upgrade technology.
Also, right now, you've got just a general sort of change in the mindset or in the zeitgeist of what's happening with security practitioners and leaders in the industry.
They're all starting to look for technology that can solve traditional problems that have historically only been solved by manpower.

Manpower is becoming increasingly more difficult to train and to maintain because nobody really wants to sit behind a security camera view all day long and stare mindlessly at an empty hallway, right?
So you end up with very low skilled employees that aren't really committed to the job.
Aren't going to be there for very long.
You're in a constant cycle of rehiring and retraining people.
It's all very expensive.
At some point, organizations will look at that and say, what are we doing?
We keep giving security thousands and thousands of dollars a year, and they keep telling us they need more money.
What is it that we need more money?
And they say, well, we need more people.
And they say, well, why do you need more people?
And they say, well, because you keep adding more facilities, we keep adding cameras to protect those facilities.
That means we have to have more eyeballs to look at those cameras, because a camera is not effective if you don't have somebody looking at it.
Those forward-thinking security leaders that have been looking at this kind of evolving over the past five years, they are saying, AI is the answer.
Now, go find me an AI product that can solve our challenges.
That's where we come in.
And we happen to be well-positioned, because most of our competition is looking at it as a feature, okay?
I need to see.

Solve the problem of a person branching a firearm, and that's what they do, and some of them do it really well, but that's kind of like hiring a security guard and then, you know, telling them that they can't look out for people falling down or they don't need to pay attention to these people that are opening this door that it's not supposed to be open because that security guard is only supposed to be looking for a person branching a firearm.
Why would you do that?
